_notes: Policy proposals are almost always Phronesis originals
level: federal | state | international | institutional — used for the level tab filter
document_type: memo | proposal | policy — used for the type filter

entries.title: A Federal AI Incident Reporting Framework
entries.subtitle: Drawing from aviation and nuclear safety models, a concrete proposal for mandatory AI incident disclosure requirements at the federal level.
entries.abstract: This proposal draws on the incident-reporting models established in aviation (NASA's ASRS) and nuclear energy (NRC's IRIS) to design a mandatory AI incident disclosure regime. It defines reportable incident categories, proposes a tiered disclosure framework, and addresses the tension between transparency and commercial confidentiality.

entries.title: Procurement as Policy: How Federal Contracting Could Shape Responsible AI
entries.subtitle: A framework for using federal procurement power to establish baseline AI safety and accountability requirements before broader legislation passes.
entries.abstract: The federal government spends billions annually on technology procurement. This paper argues that acquisition regulations — specifically FAR and DFARS — can be amended to require AI safety documentation, impact assessments, and audit rights as conditions of federal contracts, establishing baseline norms before Congress acts.
entries.preview: A framework for using federal acquisition regulations to establish AI accountability baselines as a precursor to comprehensive legislation — making procurement power do the work that statute has not yet done.

entries.title: Model Algorithmic Impact Assessment Standards for State Agencies
entries.subtitle: Draft standards for how state governments should evaluate automated decision systems before deployment in public services.
entries.abstract: This paper proposes model standards for algorithmic impact assessments that state agencies should conduct before deploying automated decision systems in public services. It covers scope, methodology, documentation requirements, public notice obligations, and appeal mechanisms, drawing on existing frameworks in Canada, the EU, and New York City.

entries.title: Transatlantic AI Governance: A Framework for U.S.-EU Regulatory Cooperation
entries.subtitle: Proposes a bilateral coordination mechanism to reduce compliance friction and establish shared standards across the Atlantic AI governance divide.
entries.abstract: The EU AI Act and emerging U.S. federal AI frameworks share broad objectives but diverge significantly in structure, scope, and enforcement. This paper proposes a bilateral coordination mechanism modeled on the EU-U.S. Trade and Technology Council to align definitions, recognize compliant certifications, and reduce duplicative compliance burdens for transatlantic AI developers.
entries.preview: A proposed bilateral coordination mechanism between the U.S. and EU that would reduce compliance friction and establish shared standards — modeled on the Trade and Technology Council but focused on AI governance.
